APT: Advanced Package Tool — the package manager used by Debian and Ubuntu-based distros to install, update, and remove software.
AUR: Arch User Repository — a community-driven repository of build scripts for Arch Linux that lets users compile and install nearly any software.
Bleeding Edge: A release model where software is updated to the very latest version as soon as it's available, before extensive testing.
Compositing: A technique where a window manager uses GPU acceleration to render transparent, animated, or layered window effects.
Container: A lightweight, isolated environment (like Docker) that runs applications without a full virtual machine, sharing the host kernel.
DE: Desktop Environment — a complete graphical interface including a window manager, panels, file manager, and system settings (e.g., GNOME, KDE).
DNF: Dandified YUM — the package manager used by Fedora and RHEL-based distros.
Flatpak: A universal sandboxed package format that works across distros, providing isolated app installations.
FOSS: Free and Open Source Software — software whose source code is freely available to view, modify, and redistribute.
GNOME: A modern desktop environment focused on simplicity, touch-friendliness, and Wayland support.
GPL: GNU General Public License — a copyleft license requiring derivative works to also be open source.
GUI: Graphical User Interface — any visual interface with windows, icons, and mouse interaction, as opposed to a text terminal.
Immutable: A system where the root filesystem is read-only, preventing accidental changes. Updates are applied atomically.
Init System: The first process (PID 1) started by the kernel, responsible for booting services. Common ones: systemd, OpenRC, runit, sysvinit.
KDE: K Desktop Environment (now KDE Plasma) — a highly customizable desktop environment with extensive settings and widgets.
Kernel: The core of the operating system that manages hardware, memory, and processes. Linux IS the kernel.
Libre: Software that respects user freedom — free to use, study, modify, and share. Stricter than just 'open source.'
LTS: Long-Term Support — a release that receives security updates for an extended period (typically 3-10 years).
Pacman: The package manager used by Arch Linux and its derivatives.
PPA: Personal Package Archive — a repository for Ubuntu-based distros that lets developers distribute software outside the official repos.
RHEL: Red Hat Enterprise Linux — a commercial enterprise distro with 10-year support cycles.
Rolling Release: A release model where the system is continuously updated rather than requiring major version upgrades.
RT Kernel: Real-Time kernel — a modified kernel that prioritizes time-sensitive tasks, essential for audio production.
Snap: Canonical's universal sandboxed package format, similar to Flatpak but tightly integrated with Ubuntu.
systemd: The most common init system on modern Linux, managing services, logging, networking, and more. Controversial for its scope.
Tiling WM: A window manager that automatically arranges windows in non-overlapping tiles, maximizing screen real estate.
Wayland: A modern display protocol replacing X11, offering better security, smoother rendering, and improved touchscreen support.
WM: Window Manager — software that controls window placement and decoration. Can be standalone (i3, Openbox) or part of a DE.
X11: The legacy display protocol for Linux GUIs. Being replaced by Wayland but still needed for some older apps.
Xfce: A lightweight desktop environment that balances features with low resource usage.
Zypper: The package manager used by openSUSE and SUSE Linux Enterprise.
